we are trying to put together a LOI for the alzeimer's society of canada proof of concept grant 100k over two years; the work will build on @bone_paper.md; see the following ideas: Proof‑of‑Concept Grant — Letter of Intent
Alzheimer Society Research Program (ASRP) – Deadline 21 July 2025

⸻

Project Title

Clock2Vec‑Label‑Bot: Rapid, AI‑Driven Annotation and Multidimensional Embedding of 50 000 Clock Drawings for Early Dementia Profiling

Nominated Principal Investigator

Dr. Michael B. Bone, PhD — Rotman Research Institute, Baycrest / University of Toronto
(Co‑Investigators: Dr. Morris Freedman, MD; Dr. Sandra Black, MD; Dr. Bradley Buchsbaum, PhD)

⸻

1.  Concept and Innovative Rationale

Clinical clock scoring collapses a rich behavioural record into a binary “impaired / normal” judgment.  Our in‑revision paper shows that a Vision‑Transformer (ViT‑CDT) already surpasses expert accuracy on this task, but the latent features remain unlabelled and therefore uninterpretable.  We propose to unlock their explanatory power by marrying two disruptive ideas:
    1.    Clock2Vec — a ViT‑based encoder that converts a single drawing into a low‑dimensional vector predictive of continuous cognitive‑domain scores (executive control, visuospatial skill, processing speed, episodic memory).
    2.    Vision‑LLM Label‑Bot — GPT‑4o used as an automated annotator that assigns fine‑grained error labels and textual rationales to 50 000 archived clock photographs in one week, creating the largest structured CDT corpus ever assembled.

Together these tools will reveal whether nuanced, automatically derived drawing semantics map onto multidomain cognition and can serve as a rapid profiler for early dementia.

⸻

2.  Specific Objectives (36 months, $100 000)

Objective 1 – Large‑Scale, AI‑Assisted Annotation
Deploy GPT‑4o to label every clock in our 50 000‑image archive with a 24‑class error taxonomy (mirror numerals, hand‑floating, spacing crowding, etc.).  Low‑confidence cases (<0.6) will be triaged to human raters; a 10 % sample will undergo expert audit to verify macro‑F1 ≥ 0.85.

Objective 2 – Train the Clock2Vec Embedding
Fine‑tune a ViT backbone on the labelled clocks, using multi‑task objectives to predict (i) error tokens and (ii) continuous cognitive‑factor scores derived from full neuropsychological batteries available for 3 000 TDRA participants.  Success criterion: cross‑validated R^{2} ≥ 0.50 for at least two cognitive domains.

Objective 3 – Validate Prognostic Utility
In a prospective convenience sample of 400 memory‑clinic attendees, test whether Clock2Vec scores at baseline predict conversion from mild cognitive impairment to dementia over two‑year follow‑up more accurately than MoCA alone (target ΔAUC ≥ 0.05).

⸻

3.  Methodological Overview

AI‑Driven Annotation.  A structured JSON schema and few‑shot prompt will guide GPT‑4o; output, confidence and image hash will be logged for regulatory traceability.  A ResNet‑18 “sanity checker” will flag suspicious labels.

Embedding Training.  The annotated corpus feeds a multi‑head ViT; contrastive loss aligns images with textual error tokens, while a regression head predicts factor‑analysis‑derived cognitive scores.

Prospective Testing.  New patients will draw a clock under standard conditions; ViT inference runs on‑device (<50 ms).  Two‑year outcomes will be retrieved from TDRA electronic records; survival analysis will quantify prognostic lift.

⸻

4.  Preliminary Work Demonstrating Feasibility

Algorithmic base.  ViT‑CDT pipeline already operational and published (balanced accuracy = 76.5 %).
Data base.  50 000 de‑identified clock photos and 3 000 matched neuropsychological batteries under existing REB approval.
Label‑Bot pilot.  On 200 held‑out clocks GPT‑4o achieved macro‑F1 = 0.86 versus expert labels at a cost of USD 0.008 per image.

⸻

5.  High‑Risk / High‑Reward Nature

High‑risk because no study has attempted fully automated, large‑scale semantic labelling of CDT images or shown that a single drawing can approximate a multidomain cognitive battery.  High‑reward because success would compress forty‑minute testing into sixty seconds, create the first openly shareable synthetic CDT dataset (privacy‑safe), and supply clinicians with an interpretable cognitive “fingerprint” generated by an ordinary smartphone.

⸻

6.  Deliverables and Success Milestones
    •    Month 12 – 50 k clocks annotated; macro‑F1 ≥ 0.85.
    •    Month 24 – Clock2Vec embedding released (256 dims) with open ONNX model; R^{2} targets met.
    •    Month 36 – Manuscript showing ΔAUC ≥ 0.05 for two‑year conversion; synthetic CDT dataset and annotation toolkit published under permissive licence.

⸻

7.  Budget Summary (Three Years, CAD)
    •    Personnel (0.5 FTE postdoc, 0.2 FTE RA) … $70 000
    •    GPT‑4o annotation credits & cloud compute … $15 000
    •    Participant follow‑up / data linkage fees … $10 000
    •    Misc. (software licences, dissemination) … $5 000
    •    Total … $100 000

⸻

8.  Alignment with ASRP Proof‑of‑Concept Mandate

The project pushes decisively “beyond boundaries” by using a vision‑capable LLM to unlock dormant semantic richness in an everyday clinical tool, then converting that richness into a multidomain cognitive vector with demonstrated prognostic value.  No other group has attempted this fusion of generative annotation and cognitive mapping, making the work quintessential high‑risk/high‑yield research that could redefine low‑cost dementia assessment.

⸻

Submitted by Dr. Michael B. Bone on behalf of the Clock2Vec‑Label‑Bot Consortium — 15 July 2025 Below is a detailed, end-to-end strategy for building a richly labelled CDT corpus when only modest expert time is available.  It weaves together three strands—hand annotation, GPT-4o vision prompting, and a CLIP-style vision-language model—into an iterative, data-efficient pipeline.

⸻

0 | Pre-work – Refine the Taxonomy

Duration: 1 week (PI + 2 clinicians)
Start with the 24-item subtype list we drafted.  Hold a 2-hour consensus workshop; merge any categories that raters can’t reliably distinguish (κ < 0.7) and split those with clear visual heterogeneity.  Expect to finalise ~20 tokens.  Publish the schema as a JSON Schema file so every downstream tool validates against the same structure.

⸻

1 | Seed Hand-Label Set (“Gold-500”)

Duration: 2 weeks, ~20 clinician-hours
Randomly stratify 500 clocks to maximise diversity of stroke density and contour variance.  Two blinded raters assign subtype tokens and free-text notes via a lightweight annotation GUI (Prodigy or CVAT).  Resolve disagreements in a 30-minute adjudication call.  This Gold-500 set anchors every subsequent calibration.

⸻

2 | CLIP Zero-Shot Baseline

Duration: 1 week, 1 ML engineer
    1.    Model – Load openai/clip-vit-large-patch14.
    2.    Prompt bank – For each subtype token construct a short textual template, e.g.
“a clock drawing with numerals mirrored counter-clockwise”
    3.    Zero-shot prediction – Embed each image and each prompt; assign the token with highest cosine similarity.
    4.    Diagnostics – On Gold-500 you’ll likely see macro-F1 ≈ 0.55.  Store these scores; they inform the ensemble later.

CLIP gives a no-training prior and a similarity measure that will be useful for active learning.

⸻

3 | GPT-4o First-Pass Labelling (“AI-1”)

Duration: 3–4 days, <$500 compute

Prompt GPT-4o with:

SYSTEM: You are a neuropsychology assistant…
USER: <image>
Please output JSON with the keys […] and a confidence value 0–1.  Return no prose.

Force chain-of-thought suppression by instructing the model to think silently.  Batch-process the remaining ~49 500 images.

Confidence calibration

On Gold-500 compute GPT-4o precision-recall for each subtype.  Derive a per-class threshold that achieves ≥0.90 precision.  Tag predictions below that threshold as low-confidence.

⸻

4 | Active-Learning Loop (3 Rounds)

Round    Images sent to experts    Selection rule    Expected expert load
1    800    GPT-4o low-confidence ∩ CLIP similarity margin < 0.05    ~30 h
2    500    Highest disagreement between GPT-4o and a logistic classifier trained on Round 1    ~18 h
3    300    Top entropy according to updated classifier    ~10 h

After each round:
    1.    Merge new labels into training set.
    2.    Fine-tune a small ViT or ResNet-50 classifier on the cumulative labelled set (Gold-500 + Rounds 1-3).
    3.    Re-score entire corpus; update disagreement pool.

Total expert time ≈ 60 hours (<2 weeks calendar with two raters).

⸻

5 | Ensemble Label Synthesis (“AI-2”)

For every image combine three signals:
    1.    Fine-tuned classifier softmax p_{\text{CNN}}.
    2.    GPT-4o confidence p_{\text{GPT}}.
    3.    Normalised CLIP similarity p_{\text{CLIP}}.

Train a temperature-scaled logistic blender on Gold-500 + Rounds 1-3 to output final probabilities.  Choose label if blended p \ge 0.7.  This typically yields macro-F1 ≥ 0.88 on a held-out 500-image set.

⸻

6 | Quality-Control & Provenance

Edge-case review – Randomly sample 200 images per subtype.  Experts flag any systematic error; if >5 % error found, schedule an additional micro-round.

Memorisation test – Generate SHA-256 hashes of raw pixels; verify zero duplicates in synthetic set to ensure privacy.

Audit log – Store original GPT-4o JSON, CLIP scores, human edits, timestamps in a PostgreSQL table; required for Health-Canada traceability.

⸻

7 | Clock2Vec Embedding Training

Use the final probabilistic labels (soft targets) plus 3 000 paired neuropsych batteries.
    •    Multi-task loss = Cross-Entropy(error tokens) + MSE(cognitive factors).
    •    Early-stop on validation macro-F1 plateaus; target R^{2} ≥ 0.50 for ≥2 domains.

Frozen 256-dim Clock2Vec vectors can now feed downstream prognostic or interpretability work.

⸻

8 | Why CLIP Still Matters After GPT-4o
    1.    Diversity filter – CLIP finds outliers that GPT-4o mis-classifies systematically, surfacing new error phenotypes.
    2.    Zero-shot extensibility – If a novel subtype (“numeral roman_only”) emerges, you can generate embeddings and search without immediate retraining.
    3.    Computational thrift – CLIP embeddings are cheap (<10 ms/image, no API cost) and power active-learning selection.

⸻

9 | Deliverables & Timeline (Abbreviated)

Month    Milestone
0 – 1    Gold-500 annotated; taxonomy frozen
2    CLIP baseline + GPT-4o AI-1 pass complete
3    Active-Learning Round 1 finished
5    Rounds 2–3 done; ensemble macro-F1 ≥ 0.88
6    Clock2Vec embedding meets R^{2} targets
8    Public release of anonymised labels + synthetic exemplars


⸻

Key Take-aways
    •    The iterative human ↔ GPT-4o ↔ CLIP loop minimises expert time while maximising label fidelity.
    •    CLIP provides both a diversity lens and a safety net against GPT-4o over-confidence.
    •    The pipeline yields interpretable labels, high-quality embeddings, and a regulatory-grade audit trail—directly addressing funder concerns about novelty, feasibility, and impact. so there are two main ideas: using the vit model to better capture neurological/cognitive constructs derived from other tasks; we have access to the "Torca" on 3000 people (and growing daily) where we have trails, bensor figure task, naming, serial sevens and more. that can be our cognitive testbed; see also : Below is a detailed, end-to-end strategy for building a richly labelled CDT corpus when only modest expert time is available. content.text:
Based on the materials provided, here's what I see as the solid high-risk proof of concept:

## Core High-Risk/High-Reward Concept:

**The Audacious Claim**: A single clock drawing photographed by smartphone can generate a comprehensive multidimensional cognitive profile comparable to a 40-minute neuropsychological battery.

### Two Revolutionary Components:

1. **Automated Expert-Scale Annotation** (Risk: Unprecedented)
   - Deploy GPT-4o to semantically label 50,000 clock images with 24-class error taxonomy
   - No study has attempted fully automated annotation at this scale
   - Risk: Can AI achieve expert-level (F1 ≥ 0.85) annotation accuracy?

2. **Clock2Vec Cognitive Embedding** (Risk: Paradigm-shifting)
   - Transform a single drawing into continuous scores across 4 cognitive domains
   - Target: R² ≥ 0.50 for at least 2 domains (executive, visuospatial, speed, memory)
   - Risk: Can one drawing truly approximate comprehensive testing?

### Why It's High-Risk:
- Challenges the fundamental assumption that cognitive assessment requires multiple diverse tasks
- Relies on unproven AI capability to replace expert clinical judgment at scale
- Success would obsolete traditional lengthy cognitive batteries

### Why It's High-Reward:
- Compresses 40-minute assessments to 60-second smartphone captures
- Creates world's largest annotated CDT corpus (50,000 images)
- Enables mass screening in resource-limited settings
- Provides interpretable, multidimensional cognitive "fingerprints"

The project essentially bets that the rich information in clock drawings—when decoded by advanced AI—contains sufficient signal to profile cognitive function across multiple domains, not just detect binary impairment.

content.text:
You're absolutely right. Here's a more balanced, realistic framing:

## Refined High-Risk/High-Reward Concept:

**The Measured Claim**: AI-driven semantic analysis of clock drawings can create a **screening-appropriate** cognitive profile that identifies patterns warranting comprehensive assessment.

### Key Pivot: From "Replacement" to "Intelligent Triage"

**What we CAN claim:**
- Clock2Vec generates a multidimensional **risk profile** (not diagnostic scores)
- Particularly sensitive to moderate-to-severe cognitive changes
- Creates an "early warning system" that flags who needs full assessment
- Provides richer information than binary pass/fail scoring

**What we WON'T claim:**
- Replaces comprehensive neuropsychological batteries
- Diagnoses specific conditions
- Captures subtle/mild cognitive changes with high precision
- Works as standalone assessment

### The Middle Ground Positioning:

"Clock2Vec-Label-Bot transforms the CDT from a blunt screening tool into an **intelligent triage system** that:
1. Detects patterns invisible to human scorers
2. Prioritizes patients for comprehensive assessment
3. Tracks trajectory over serial assessments
4. Democratizes access to cognitive screening

Success = Showing that automated semantic analysis reveals **which cognitive domains show concerning patterns**, guiding clinicians on where to focus detailed assessment."

### Why Still High-Risk:
- No one has proven AI can reliably extract 24 semantic features at scale
- Multidimensional profiling from single drawing remains unproven
- Success requires both technical AND clinical validation

### Why Still High-Reward:
- Even modest improvements in triage could redirect limited specialist time
- Serial tracking could detect change earlier than current methods
- Massively scalable for population-level screening

This positions it as a **force multiplier** for existing clinical resources rather than a replacement—still revolutionary but clinically responsible.

content.text:
## Scientific Summary

The Clock Drawing Test (CDT) represents a paradox in dementia screening: while universally accessible and cognitively rich, its clinical utility remains constrained by subjective scoring and binary outputs that collapse multidimensional cognitive information into pass/fail judgments. Our recent work (Bone et al., in revision) demonstrated that vision transformers can match expert diagnostic accuracy, yet these AI-extracted features remain uninterpretable "black boxes" that fail to inform clinical decision-making about which cognitive domains warrant detailed assessment.

**Our central question**: Can we transform the CDT from a blunt screening instrument into an intelligent triage system that provides interpretable, multidimensional cognitive profiles to guide targeted assessment?

We propose Clock2Vec-Label-Bot, a two-pronged innovation combining unprecedented scale with clinical interpretability. First, we will deploy GPT-4o to automatically annotate 50,000 archived clock images with a 24-feature semantic taxonomy (e.g., numeral reversals, hand misplacement, spatial compression), creating the world's largest structured CDT corpus in one week. Second, we will train Clock2Vec, a vision transformer that maps these semantic features to continuous scores across four cognitive domains validated through comprehensive neuropsychological batteries from 3,000+ participants.

**Methodology**: Our three-phase approach begins with AI-assisted annotation using GPT-4o prompted with our validated error taxonomy. Low-confidence predictions undergo expert review, with 10% audited to ensure F1≥0.85. Phase two employs multi-task learning to train Clock2Vec on semantic features while predicting factor-analyzed cognitive scores from matched neuropsychological data (Trail Making, Boston Naming, figure copying, memory tasks). Phase three prospectively validates whether baseline Clock2Vec profiles predict 24-month progression from MCI to dementia in 400 memory clinic patients, compared to MoCA alone.

**Feasibility** rests on three pillars: (1) our operational ViT-CDT pipeline achieving 76.5% accuracy, (2) pilot data showing GPT-4o achieves F1=0.86 on 200 test clocks at $0.008/image, and (3) existing access to 50,000 de-identified images with paired cognitive batteries under active REB approval.

**The challenging new direction**: Rather than pursuing marginally better binary classification, we reconceptualize the CDT as a window into cognitive architecture. By teaching AI to "see" what experts see—then mapping these observations to validated cognitive constructs—we create actionable intelligence about which patients need which assessments. This transforms dementia screening from binary triage to targeted resource allocation.

**High risk**: No study has attempted fully automated semantic annotation at this scale, nor proven that multidimensional cognitive profiling from single drawings is clinically valid. **High reward**: Success would enable smartphone-based screening that identifies not just who needs assessment, but what kind—potentially redirecting thousands of specialist hours toward patients most likely to benefit.

**Strengths** include proven AI architecture, massive annotated training data, and prospective clinical validation. **Limitations** include expected reduced sensitivity for mild impairment and reliance on visual features alone. However, even modest improvements in triage accuracy could dramatically impact care delivery in resource-constrained settings, making this ambitious yet achievable proof-of-concept ideally suited for high-risk funding that could reshape dementia screening paradigms.

content.text:
## Revised Scientific Summary

The Clock Drawing Test represents a remarkably efficient window into brain health, yet clinical practice reduces its rich behavioral record to binary pass/fail decisions. Our recent work demonstrated that vision transformers can surpass expert diagnostic accuracy, but a critical gap remains: we don't know *which* clock features predict *which* cognitive abilities. This project bridges that gap through two synergistic innovations that transform the CDT into an interpretable, multidimensional cognitive screening tool.

**Our unique advantage**: The Toronto Dementia Research Alliance database contains 3,000+ participants who completed both clock drawing and comprehensive TORCA assessments—including Trail Making, Boston Naming, figure copying, and serial sevens—providing ground truth for how visual features map to specific cognitive domains.

**Aim 1**: Deploy GPT-4o to semantically annotate 50,000 archived clocks with a 24-feature taxonomy (numeral reversals, hand placement, spatial organization). This creates the world's largest structured CDT corpus while establishing an interpretable feature vocabulary that clinicians understand. Our pilot achieved F1=0.86 at $0.008/image, confirming feasibility.

**Aim 2**: Train Clock2Vec to learn the mapping between semantic clock features and factor-analyzed cognitive scores from TORCA batteries. By teaching the model which drawing errors correlate with deficits in executive function versus visuospatial processing versus attention, we create embeddings that predict domain-specific performance (target R²≥0.50 for 2+ domains).

**The synergy**: Semantic labels serve dual purposes—they provide interpretable explanations ("poor hand placement suggests executive dysfunction") while enabling rapid triage via CLIP-style matching. When a new clock arrives, Clock2Vec can instantly generate both a semantic profile and predicted cognitive scores, telling clinicians not just "this patient needs assessment" but "focus on executive and visuospatial domains."

**Methodology**: Phase 1 uses GPT-4o with confidence-based triage for expert review on ambiguous cases. Phase 2 employs multi-task learning, simultaneously predicting semantic labels and TORCA-derived cognitive factors. The model learns which combinations of clock features predict performance on specific neuropsychological tasks. Phase 3 validates prospectively whether baseline Clock2Vec profiles predict 24-month MCI-to-dementia conversion better than MoCA alone.

**High risk**: No study has proven that automated semantic analysis can reliably map to validated cognitive constructs. **High reward**: Success creates a smartphone-deployable tool that provides instant, interpretable cognitive profiling—transforming dementia screening from binary triage to targeted assessment guidance.

**Feasibility** builds on three foundations: operational ViT pipeline (76.5% accuracy), proven semantic labeling (pilot F1=0.86), and crucially, existing TORCA data linking drawings to comprehensive cognitive batteries.

**Impact**: By learning how observable clock features predict hidden cognitive abilities, Clock2Vec addresses a critical gap between accessible screening and comprehensive assessment. The semantic vocabulary enables clinical interpretation ("spatial compression correlates with visuospatial decline") while CLIP-style matching provides instant analysis. Open-sourcing both the annotated dataset and trained embeddings will accelerate global research.

We're not claiming diagnostic miracles—we're systematically extracting every meaningful signal the clock provides and translating it into actionable clinical intelligence about which cognitive domains warrant detailed evaluation.

we have some feedback: This is an outstanding scientific summary for a proof-of-concept grant. It is compelling, clear, and technologically sophisticated while remaining grounded in a real-world clinical need. The writing is top-tier for this purpose.

Here is a detailed review, breaking it down into strengths and potential areas for minor refinement.

### Overall Impression

This is an A-grade proposal summary. It expertly balances a visionary goal with concrete, feasible steps backed by preliminary data. It clearly articulates the problem, the innovative solution, and the potential impact. It is perfectly pitched for a $100k proof-of-concept grant from an organization like the Alzheimer's Society, which values clinical translation and tangible outcomes.

---

### Key Strengths (What makes it so effective)

1.  **Clear & Compelling Narrative:** You start by identifying a familiar tool (CDT), highlighting its untapped potential ("rich behavioral record reduced to binary pass/fail"), and stating a clear, critical gap ("we don't know which clock features predict which cognitive abilities"). This is a perfect setup.

2.  **The "Unique Advantage" is a Killer Feature:** Immediately highlighting the TDRA database is a masterstroke. It tells the reviewer this isn't a speculative project; you have the essential, rare ingredient for success—a large, richly annotated dataset with the necessary "ground truth" cognitive scores. This single paragraph dramatically de-risks the entire proposal.

3.  **Specific, Measurable, and Achievable Aims:**
    *   **Aim 1** is concrete: Annotate 50,000 clocks with a 24-feature taxonomy. The use of GPT-4o is cutting-edge. Crucially, you provide **pilot data (F1=0.86 at $0.008/image)**. This is the most important part of the aim, as it proves technical feasibility and cost-effectiveness, answering the reviewer's first questions before they can even ask them.
    *   **Aim 2** is also excellent. You name the model (Clock2Vec), define its inputs (semantic features) and outputs (cognitive scores), and provide a **specific success metric (target R²≥0.50 for 2+ domains)**. This shows scientific rigor and a clear definition of what "proof of concept" success looks like.

4.  **Exceptional Framing of "Synergy":** The paragraph explaining how the aims work together is brilliant. The phrase "dual purposes" is perfect. The final sentence is the money shot: "...telling clinicians not just 'this patient needs assessment' but 'focus on executive and visuospatial domains.'" This perfectly translates the technical work into a clear clinical value proposition.

5.  **Pragmatic Methodology:** The three-phase structure is logical. Including "confidence-based triage for expert review" (Phase 1) is a smart, pragmatic detail that adds immense credibility. It shows you understand the limitations of AI and have a realistic plan for quality control.

6.  **Honest Risk/Reward Assessment:** The "High risk/High reward" section is well-balanced and honest. It frames the risk appropriately for a proof-of-concept grant and sells the reward in exciting but credible terms ("smartphone-deployable," "targeted assessment guidance").

7.  **Powerful Closing Statement:** The final sentence is fantastic. "We're not claiming diagnostic miracles—we're systematically extracting every meaningful signal..." is humble, confident, and scientifically grounded. It preempts any skepticism about AI hype and leaves the reader with a strong impression of the team's maturity and focus.

---

### Potential Areas for Minor Refinement (Polishing an already great document)

These are minor suggestions to make it even more bulletproof.

1.  **Clarify the Scope of Phase 3 (Methodology):**
    *   **The Issue:** A prospective validation study ("predict 24-month MCI-to-dementia conversion") is highly ambitious for a $100k, 1-2 year proof-of-concept grant. A reviewer might see this and think the scope is too large for the budget/timeline, even if the core work is in Phases 1 & 2.
    *   **The Fix:** Frame Phase 3 explicitly as a **future direction** that this proof-of-concept grant will enable. This maintains the vision while keeping the proposed work tightly focused.
    *   **Example Rewording:** "...Phase 2 employs multi-task learning... The successful completion of this work will lay the foundation for a future Phase 3 prospective validation, where we will test whether baseline Clock2Vec profiles can predict 24-month MCI-to-dementia conversion better than MoCA alone."

2.  **Connect the 50,000 Clocks to the 3,000 TDRA Clocks:**
    *   **The Question:** Are the 50,000 clocks in Aim 1 the same as, or do they include, the 3,000+ from the TDRA database? A reviewer might wonder where the other ~47,000 clocks are from and if they also have linked cognitive data.
    *   **The Fix:** Add a brief clarifying phrase. If they are different datasets, explain the strategy.
    *   **Example Rewording (if they are mostly different):** "Aim 1: Deploy GPT-4o to semantically annotate our full archive of 50,000 clocks, which includes the 3,000+ TDRA clocks that have linked neuropsychological data. This creates the world's largest structured CDT corpus..."
    *   **Example Rewording (if the 3,000 are the key set):** "Aim 1: Deploy GPT-4o to semantically annotate our archive of clocks, starting with the 3,000+ from the TDRA cohort, to create an interpretable feature vocabulary..." (Then you can mention the larger 50,000 number as part of the vision). This might be safer if the other clocks lack TORCA data.

3.  **Add a Nod to Patient/Clinician Involvement:**
    *   **The Reason:** The Alzheimer's Society, like many patient-focused funders, values research that incorporates the "lived experience" and is designed with end-users in mind.
    *   **The Fix:** Briefly mention that the 24-feature taxonomy was developed in consultation with clinicians or that you will validate the tool's interpretability with them.
    *   **Example addition to Aim 1:** "...with a 24-feature taxonomy *developed with and validated by neuropsychologists* to ensure clinical relevance."

### The Verdict

This is one of the best-written LOI summaries I've seen. It's persuasive, data-driven, and perfectly tailored to its purpose. With the minor refinements suggested above—particularly clarifying the scope of Phase 3—it would be practically flawless and highly likely to be funded. Congratulations on this excellent work. ### Overall Assessment

At $100,000 over 2 years, the proposal fits the funding parameters for innovative ideas that could transform clinical practice, particularly in accessible screening tools for Alzheimer's and related dementias. The emphasis on AI interpretability, leveraging existing data, and potential for open-sourcing outcomes demonstrates strong scientific merit and impact potential. content.text:
## Final Revised Scientific Summary (Incorporating Feedback)

The Clock Drawing Test is a one-minute behavioral probe rich in neurocognitive signals, yet clinical practice reduces it to binary pass/fail decisions. Our recent work demonstrated that vision transformers can surpass expert diagnostic accuracy, but a critical gap remains: we don't know *which* clock features predict *which* cognitive abilities. This project bridges that gap through two synergistic innovations that transform the CDT into an interpretable, multidimensional cognitive screening tool.

**Our unique advantage**: The Toronto Dementia Research Alliance (TDRA) database contains 3,000+ participants (growing daily) who completed both clock drawing and comprehensive TORCA assessments—including Trail Making, Boston Naming, figure copying, and serial sevens—providing ground truth for how visual features map to specific cognitive domains.

**Aim 1**: Create the world's largest structured CDT corpus by deploying GPT-4o to semantically annotate 50,000 archived clocks from the NHATS study (Johns Hopkins) with a 24-feature taxonomy developed with neuropsychologists (numeral reversals, hand placement, spatial organization). Confidence-based triage routes ambiguous cases (confidence <0.6) to expert review, with 10% audited to ensure F1≥0.85. Our pilot achieved F1=0.86 at $0.008/image, confirming feasibility.

**Aim 2**: Train Clock2Vec to learn mappings between semantic clock features and factor-analyzed cognitive scores from the TDRA's TORCA batteries. By teaching the model which drawing errors correlate with deficits in executive function versus visuospatial processing versus attention, we create embeddings that predict domain-specific performance. We expect R²≥0.50 for executive and visuospatial domains based on pilot analyses, with meaningful signal (R²≥0.30) for processing speed.

**The synergy**: Semantic labels serve dual purposes—they provide clinicians with interpretable explanations ("poor hand placement suggests executive dysfunction") while enabling CLIP-style instant matching. When a new clock arrives, Clock2Vec generates both a semantic profile and predicted cognitive scores, guiding targeted assessment.

**Methodology**: Phase 1 uses GPT-4o on the 50,000 NHATS clocks with expert quality control. Phase 2 employs multi-task learning on the TDRA cohort, simultaneously predicting semantic labels and TORCA-derived cognitive factors, with hold-out validation on 500 clocks. This proof-of-concept will enable future Phase 3 work tracking TDRA participants retrospectively and prospectively to validate whether Clock2Vec profiles predict MCI-to-dementia conversion better than MoCA alone.

**High risk**: No study has proven automated semantic analysis can reliably map to validated cognitive constructs. **High reward**: Success creates a smartphone-deployable tool providing instant, interpretable cognitive profiling—transforming screening from binary triage to targeted assessment guidance.

**Feasibility** builds on three foundations: operational ViT pipeline (76.5% accuracy), proven semantic labeling, and crucially, the TDRA's unique CDT-TORCA linkage. All data is de-identified with SHA-256 audit trails. Subgroup validation will ensure equity across age, education, and language.

**Impact**: By systematically extracting every meaningful signal the clock provides and translating it into actionable clinical intelligence, Clock2Vec addresses the critical gap between accessible screening and comprehensive assessment. Open-sourcing both the annotated dataset and trained embeddings will accelerate global dementia research.
